stories.headline: Parent group sues Denver Public Schools, alleges district defrauded voters with bond measures
stories.summary:
A parent advocacy group called Mamás de DPS filed a lawsuit Tuesday in Denver District Court accusing Denver Public Schools of defrauding taxpayers by misleading them about the purpose of voter-approved bond measures, including a nearly $1 billion bond passed two years ago. The suit alleges racketeering and violations of the Colorado Organized Crime Control Act through a scheme to generate debt in violation of state law.

The lawsuit claims DPS used a nonprofit called Denver School Facilities Leasing Corporation to obtain titles and mortgages for 33 school buildings, then leased the buildings back to DPS, which allowed the nonprofit to mortgage the buildings through a bank trustee — Wells Fargo — in exchange for debt proceeds. Mamás de DPS attorney Lisi Owen argues the district used bond proceeds to pay off non-voter-approved debt and misled voters about why the money was needed.

DPS spokesman Scott Pribble called the suit 'another frivolous lawsuit' and defended the district's financing methods as 'standard and widely accepted' public finance used by school districts across Colorado. The group had previously filed a 2024 lawsuit seeking to halt school closures, and Owen said the district's responses in that case prompted the new filing.

The case adds significant legal pressure on DPS at a time when the district is already managing declining enrollment and contentious school closure decisions. Courts have previously upheld the constitutionality of the certificates-of-participation financing method the district employs, according to DPS.

stories.headline: Douglas County wildfire sparked by trash truck, sheriff says; more than 1,000 acres burned near Franktown
stories.summary:
A trash truck that dropped a 'hot load' at an unspecified location south of Franktown ignited the Dahlberg Fire on Tuesday, which burned through 1,081 acres of dry, grassy land in Douglas County, according to the Douglas County Sheriff's Office. The fire was first reported near Dahlberg and Lake Gulch roads at 12:39 p.m., roughly 8 miles south of Franktown and 8 miles east of Larkspur.

Dry vegetation and winds of up to 20 mph allowed the fire to spread rapidly. More than a dozen homes and the nearby Cherry Valley Elementary School were evacuated as a precaution, though no property damage was reported and residents were allowed to return home after the fire was fully contained by Tuesday evening.

Crews remained on scene overnight to mop up hotspots and monitor for any rekindling under continued high wind conditions. The sheriff's office did not specify the reason the truck dropped the hot load.

The fire comes amid historically low snowpack across Colorado. As of February 25, the state's snowpack stood at 63 percent of normal for the date, with a snow-water equivalent of 7.8 inches — tying a record low for that date set in 2002.

stories.headline: Colorado congressional delegation splits sharply along party lines in reaction to Trump's State of the Union address
stories.summary:
Colorado's congressional delegation responded to President Donald Trump's State of the Union address Tuesday along strict partisan lines, with the state's four Republican House members praising the speech and six Democrats denouncing it as false and divisive. The address lasted nearly two hours and covered immigration, energy production, and the administration's first-year agenda.

Republican U.S. Rep. Gabe Evans called it a 'pivotal moment,' while Rep. Jeff Crank of Colorado Springs posted 'The State of the Union is STRONG!' and Rep. Lauren Boebert credited Trump with securing the border and boosting energy production. Rep. Jeff Hurd, who lost Trump's endorsement over tariff differences, struck a more measured tone, focusing on fiscal discipline and support for small businesses.

Democratic U.S. Sen. John Hickenlooper predicted ahead of the speech that Trump would tell Americans 'not to believe what we can see with our own eyes,' citing unaffordability and what he described as lawless ICE activity. Rep. Jason Crow of Aurora, one of several lawmakers the Justice Department unsuccessfully attempted to indict over a video advising troops not to follow illegal orders, attended the speech and sat with fellow targets of the effort.

Rep. Diana DeGette of Denver was the only delegation member who did not attend, saying on social media she chose not to 'legitimize' what she described as the president's 'egregious' actions. The sharp divide reflects the broader national political polarization ahead of 2026 midterm elections.

stories.headline: Culinary Creative Group CEO Juan Padro steps down; service charge lawsuit moves toward trial
stories.summary:
Juan Padro, co-founder of Denver restaurant group Culinary Creative Group, is stepping down as chief executive officer after more than 15 years leading the company, which operates more than a dozen concepts across the metro area including Señor Bear, Mister Oso, Tap & Burger, and A5 Steakhouse. Richard Flaherty, whose most recent role was CEO of Punch Bowl Social, will succeed Padro.

Padro is remaining with the company as a consultant and retains an equity stake. The transition was described as a 'purposeful evolution' planned three years in advance, intended to position Culinary Creative Group for national expansion while preserving its founding identity. Blake Edmunds was named chief culinary director and Lauren Barash was appointed the company's first vice president of marketing.

The leadership change coincides with an ongoing lawsuit filed in February 2025 by a former server at Culinary Creative's Kumoya restaurant. Marianna White originally alleged the company was retaining employee tips under the guise of service charges; she later amended the complaint to focus on the legal distinction between service charges and tips.

Denver District Court Judge Sarah Block Wallace declined this week to pause the case pending a legal clarification, saying she would set a trial date and address the service charge question at an upcoming case management conference. The outcome could have broader implications for how Denver restaurants disclose and distribute mandatory fees charged to customers.

stories.headline: Early March snowstorm could bring up to 12 inches to Denver; forecasts remain uncertain
stories.summary:
A potential significant snowstorm is being tracked for the Denver metro area in early March, with forecast models from Pivotal Weather projecting up to 12 inches of snow over a 24-hour period around March 5-6, though updated modeling as of February 26 shifts the window to March 6-8 and reduces the single-storm total to approximately 10 inches. Over the full stretch through March 12, some models project 14 inches of cumulative snowfall.

Fort Collins is forecast to receive around 9 inches and Castle Rock approximately 10 inches in the same event, while Colorado Springs would see lesser accumulations near 5.5 inches. The storm, if it materializes, would come after a winter that has largely spared the Front Range from significant snowfall.

Forecasters cautioned that with more than a week of lead time, considerable uncertainty remains. AccuWeather models suggest temperatures in Denver could be too warm for substantial snow accumulation during the same period, making the forecast a 'wait-and-see' scenario.

The storm's potential significance is amplified by historically poor snowpack conditions statewide. Colorado's snowpack sat at 63 percent of normal as of February 25, with a snow-water equivalent that ties a record low for that date set in 2002, raising concerns about water supplies heading into spring and summer.

stories.headline: Greenwood Village firm refinances 238-unit Denver apartment complex near Belleview Station with $40 million loan
stories.summary:
Griffis Residential, a Greenwood Village-based multifamily real estate company, has refinanced its 238-unit apartment community near Belleview Station in Denver with a $40 million loan arranged through JLL. The transit-oriented property is situated near the light rail station that connects residents to downtown Denver and other metro destinations.

The refinancing signals continued institutional confidence in Denver's multifamily housing market, particularly in properties with proximity to the Regional Transportation District's light rail network. Transit-oriented developments have drawn sustained investor interest as Denver works to increase density along rail corridors.

The deal comes as Denver is also in the process of revising its energy code, soliciting input from developers and community members to align building standards with the city's climate goals while addressing industry concerns about the regulatory burden on new construction and renovation projects.

The Belleview Station area has been a focus of significant multifamily development in recent years, with the station anchoring a mixed-use district in the Denver Tech Center corridor that includes retail, office, and residential uses.

stories.headline: Arvada Center names Denver Zoo executive Noelle DeLage as next president and CEO
stories.summary:
The Arvada Center for the Arts and Humanities has named Noelle DeLage as its next president and chief executive officer, the organization announced. DeLage currently serves as chief communications and philanthropy officer of the Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance and will assume her new role effective July 1.

DeLage succeeds Philip Sneed, who is retiring after leading the Arvada Center for a significant tenure. Her appointment comes as the Arvada Center prepares for what the organization described as the beginning of a new phase of activity and programming.

The Arvada Center is one of the largest multidisciplinary arts centers in Colorado, operating gallery spaces, a history museum, outdoor amphitheater, and indoor theater stages that draw audiences from across the Denver metro area. The transition in leadership is expected to shape the organization's strategic direction for years to come.

DeLage's background combines nonprofit communications, fundraising, and conservation advocacy. Her experience at the Denver Zoo, a major metro cultural institution, positions her to manage both the programmatic and philanthropic dimensions of the Arvada Center's operations.

stories.headline: Sandoitchi Japanese sandwich pop-up returns to Denver's RiNo neighborhood through March 7
stories.summary:
Sandoitchi, a Dallas-based pop-up known for elevated Japanese-style sandwiches, has returned to Denver's RiNo neighborhood for a two-week run at The Source Hotel, open daily from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. through Saturday, March 7. The concept, which launched in 2020 during a pandemic-era surge in Japanese 'sando' popularity, last visited Denver in 2024.

The menu features classic offerings such as the Strawberry Cream Sando, Egg Salad Sando, and Classic Chicken Katsu Sando, alongside new items including a Wagyu Burger Katsu Sando, a Curry Ebi Shrimp Katsu Sando, and Japanese-inspired sides. Drinks include matcha options, a Yuzu Arnold Palmer, and a Manjo Ube Lassi.

Chef Stevie Nguyen, who has cooked at Morimoto and Momofuku Ko in New York, is the culinary force behind the sandwiches, which are made from Japanese milk bread using a tangzhong technique that produces a distinctive soft, feathery texture. Business partner Keith Tran cited Denver as 'a really dynamic food city' and a favored market for the traveling pop-up.

The Source Hotel in RiNo has become a recurring destination for food-focused pop-up concepts, and the Sandoitchi return is expected to draw significant interest from Denver's food community given the pop-up's national following.
